Curriculum → High-level design interview prep → Foundations and estimation

HLD learning path

Foundations and estimation

Build the habit of turning vague prompts into concrete requirements, scale assumptions, and explicit trade-offs.

Module progress 0%

0 of 5 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: Problem framing and requirements

Start every design with the user journey, hard constraints, and the two or three metrics that will define success.

Why this module matters Build one clean mental model before scaling it.

Move through the lessons in order so each trade-off has context before the harder distributed systems decisions show up.

Study target 5 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

High-level design interview prep 5 lessons 0/5 complete Follow in order for best results Back to HLD roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the summary, inspect the diagram, and identify the sharpest trade-off.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
HLD sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Frame product requirements before picking components
  • Estimate traffic, storage, and throughput with quick math
  • Explain performance, scalability, consistency, and availability in interviewer-friendly language

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core trade-off in your own words.
  • Study the diagram and identify the critical path or failure boundary.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear assumptions and estimated scale.
  • Justified component choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of operations, failure modes, and trade-offs.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

0/5 complete
Lesson 1 Next
Problem framing and requirements

Start every design with the user journey, hard constraints, and the two or three metrics that will define success.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 2 Open
Back-of-the-envelope estimation

Use quick arithmetic to size read/write traffic, storage growth, cache footprint, and inter-service bandwidth before deep diving.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 3 Open
Performance vs. scalability

Performance describes how fast one request path is now; scalability describes how gracefully the system behaves as load, data, or team size grows.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 4 Open
Latency, throughput, and SLOs

Reason about how much work the system can do, how long each request takes, and which percentile targets users actually feel.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 5 Open
Availability, consistency, and CAP trade-offs

Designs under partition must choose whether to prioritize fresh coordinated answers or continued service with potentially stale data.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

Problem framing and requirements

Open

Start every design with the user journey, hard constraints, and the two or three metrics that will define success.

Why it matters

Strong interview answers feel structured because the requirements phase narrows the search space before any component is introduced.

What you will cover

What to ask firstTranslate goals into system constraintsShape the rest of the conversation

Good interview signal

  • Open with user-facing requirements and non-functional constraints.
  • State traffic assumptions numerically, even if they are rough.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

Back-of-the-envelope estimation

Open

Use quick arithmetic to size read/write traffic, storage growth, cache footprint, and inter-service bandwidth before deep diving.

Why it matters

Interviewers care less about perfect numbers than about whether your estimates are directionally correct and connected to design decisions.

What you will cover

Estimate the steady stateUse mental reference numbersTie estimates to architecture choices

Good interview signal

  • Calculate average and peak QPS.
  • Estimate storage growth for 1 day and 1 year.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Performance vs. scalability

Open

Performance describes how fast one request path is now; scalability describes how gracefully the system behaves as load, data, or team size grows.

Why it matters

Good designs keep today’s latency reasonable while preserving low-friction ways to add capacity later.

What you will cover

Performance is about the current experienceScalability is about shape under growthUse both in trade-off language

Good interview signal

  • Differentiate fast now from easy to grow later.
  • Name which component becomes the bottleneck first.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 4

Latency, throughput, and SLOs

Open

Reason about how much work the system can do, how long each request takes, and which percentile targets users actually feel.

Why it matters

This is the language that connects product promises to capacity planning, queues, caches, and admission control.

What you will cover

Latency is the user-visible delayThroughput is sustained work per unit timeSLOs drive trade-offs

Good interview signal

  • Use percentile latency, not only averages.
  • Call out both average load and peak concurrency.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 5

Availability, consistency, and CAP trade-offs

Open

Designs under partition must choose whether to prioritize fresh coordinated answers or continued service with potentially stale data.

Why it matters

Many interview prompts become easier when you can place each operation on the spectrum between strict correctness and graceful degradation.

What you will cover

Use precise definitionsRead CAP correctlyExplain the user impact

Good interview signal

  • State the consistency requirement per critical operation.
  • Describe the system behavior during partition, failover, and recovery.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
